title: 2022 Alumni Award of Merit: Julie E. Buring SD '83
text:
ingesting omega-3 fatty acids Vitamin D
supplements migraines and stroke risk
these and many other widely accepted
influences on Health and Longevity can
be traced back to the important work of
Dr Julie Elizabeth burring
since earning her doctor of Science in
epidemiology from the Harvard t.h Chan
School of Public Health in 1983. Dr
burring has conducted groundbreaking
Research into the epidemiology of
chronic diseases with a focus on
cardiovascular disease in cancer in
women
as a clinical investigator and
passionate educator she has made a
profound impact on the field of
epidemiology for almost four decades
her contributions to her home
institutions Brigham and Women's
Hospital Harvard Medical School and
Harvard Chan school have been exemplary
the courses she developed and taught at
Harvard Chan school and Harvard Medical
School on epidemiology and randomized
clinical trials have helped educate the
next generation of health professionals
locally nationally and around the world
as an educator Dr burring has been
honored with the Roger L Nichols
excellence and teaching award from
Harvard Chan School twice in 2002 and
2007 as well as the 2021 William silent
Lifetime Achievement and mentoring award
from Harvard Medical School also in 2021
she received the Barbara J McNeil
faculty award for exceptional
institutional service to Harvard Medical
School
over the course of her career Dr burring
has been the Principal investigator or
co-investigator on several large-scale
randomized clinical trials many of which
attracted media attention and spurred
the acceptance of new health practices
these include
the Physicians health study which
revealed that regular doses of aspirin
reduce the rate of heart attacks by 44
percent in men
the women's health study which found
that in the case of women aspirin
reduced the risk of stroke in women but
not heart attacks and the vital study
which revealed that regular doses of
Vitamin D could reduce cancer-related
deaths and that omega-3 fish oils reduce
the risk of heart attack
building on her work Dr burring served
as the founding director of clinical
research at the osher center for
integrative medicine at Brigham and
Women's Hospital and Harvard Medical
School where her team found ways to
credibly test the effectiveness of
alternative medicine and advocated for
more transparent doctor-patient
communication about complementary and
Integrative Health
one study at osher examined the rate of
patients who disclosed complementary and
Integrative Health use to their
Physicians the results showed that
approximately 20 percent of patients
would use complementary and Integrative
Health within the last 12 months had not
discussed it with their physician and 58
percent said this was because their
physician did not ask
Dr burring argued that this lack of
communication results in missed
opportunities for Physicians to safely
advise on proven complementary and
Integrative Health treatments
the impact of Dr burring's work cannot
be understated her research which has
been widely published including in the
New England Journal of Medicine and the
Journal of the National Cancer Institute
has significantly contributed to the
development and use of clinical trials
over the past several decades
[Music]
for her contributions to the
epidemiology of chronic disease and
dedication to educating the next
generation of scientific leaders the
Harvard t.h Chan School of Public Health
recognizes Julie Elizabeth burring with
the 2022 alumni Award of Merit
[Music]
